13 min read
3 hours ago

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Using a Large Language Model (LLM) in an application isn’t just about sending a question and getting a response. The real challenge is making sure you ask the right question and then correctly handle the answer the LLM gives you.
Think of it like this:
Asking the Right Question: You need to carefully craft the “prompt” you send to the LLM. This prompt needs to be clear, specific, and structured in a way that guides the model to give you the best possible answer. This is the input.
Handling the Answer: Once the LLM gives you a response, you need to be able to process it correctly to get the final result you want for your application. This could mean cleaning it up, extracting specific information, or formatting it in a certain way. This is the output.
LangChain is a tool designed to solve this exact problem. It provides building blocks that help you manage both the input (crafting the prompt) and the output (processing the prediction) so you can effectively use LLMs in your applications, whether they are simple or complex.
Why LangChain?
You can build LLM applications without LangChain, often by using the direct tools (SDKs) from providers like OpenAI. However, using LangChain offers two big advantages that will help you in the long run:
1. It comes with pre-built solutions.
LangChain includes ready-made templates for common LLM tasks like thinking through a problem step by step (Chain-of-Thought) or using tools. This means you don’t have to start from scratch. You can use these patterns out of the box and only make changes if you need to.
2. It has interchangeable parts.
All the components in LangChain like the LLM itself, the embedding models, and vector stores are built to a shared standard. This means you can easily swap out one part for another.
For Example:
If you start with OpenAI’s LLM but later want to try a different commercial one like Anthropic or an open-source one like a model from Ollama, you can just plug it in without re-writing your entire application.
The same goes for embedding models (which convert text into a numerical format) and vector stores (databases that store those numbers). You can easily switch from OpenAI to Cohere for embeddings, or from PGVector to Weaviate for your database.
Resources :-

• https://python.langchain.com/docs/integrations/chat/

• https://python.langchain.com/docs/integrations/text_embedding/

• https://python.langchain.com/docs/integrations/vectorstores/
This modular design prevents you from being “locked in” to a single provider. It ensures your application is flexible and can adapt as new and better models or tools become available.

Now let’s do some coding. For that, we first need to set up. Let’s see how you can set up LangChain.
Using LLMs in LangChain
At its core, a Large Language Model (LLM) is the brain behind most generative AI applications. When you’re working with LangChain, it gives you two simple ways to connect with any LLM provider, including Google’s Gemini:
- Chat Models
- LLMs
The `LLM` interface is the most straightforward. You simply pass it a text prompt, and it sends that prompt to the model and gives you back a prediction.
Let’s see how that works with a Gemini LLM wrapper from LangChain:
step 1: install the package
`npm install @langchain/google-genai`
Step 2: writing code
import { ChatGoogleGenerativeAI } from "@langchain/google-genai";
import "dotenv/config";
const model = new ChatGoogleGenerativeAI({
model: "gemini-2.5-flash",
apiKey: process.env.GEMINI_API_KEY, // Make sure you set this in your .env
});const response = await model.invoke("the green tea is");
console.log(response);
output
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Press enter or click to view image in full size

This is one of the most important settings to adjust when you’re using an LLM or a chat model. Most providers offer several models, each with different trade-offs in terms of capability and cost. Generally, the larger models are more powerful but also more expensive and a bit slower.
For instance, Google has different Gemini models like `gemini-pro`, `gemini-pro-vision`, and `gemini-2.5-flash` for various tasks.
There are also a couple of other handy parameters you can tweak, which are available with most providers:
temperature: This controls how the model “samples” its output. A lower value (like 0.1) gives you more predictable, focused results. A higher value (like 0.9) makes the output more creative and unexpected. You’ll want to choose a value based on your task. For structured tasks (like data extraction), a low temperature is best, while creative writing benefits from a higher one.
max_tokens: This sets a limit on the size and cost of the model’s response. Be careful with this one if you set it too low, the model might cut off its answer before it’s finished.
Remember, each provider has its own unique set of parameters. It’s always a good idea to check the specific documentation for the provider you’re using.

The Chat Model Interface
Alternatively, the chat model interface is designed for back-and-forth conversations. The reason it’s a separate interface is that popular LLM providers like Google and OpenAI treat messages differently, assigning them to specific roles:
1. User Role: This is for messages and content coming from the user.
2. Assistant Role: This is for the content generated by the AI model.
3. System Role: This is for giving instructions to the model that it should follow throughout the conversation.
The chat model interface in LangChain makes it much simpler to manage these conversations, which is perfect for building AI chatbots.
Using LangChain’s `ChatGoogleGenerativeAI` model:
import { ChatGoogleGenerativeAI } from "@langchain/google-genai";
import "dotenv/config";import { HumanMessage } from "@langchain/core/messages";
const model = new ChatGoogleGenerativeAI({
model: "gemini-2.5-flash",
apiKey: process.env.GEMINI_API_KEY,
});
const prompt = [new HumanMessage("What is the capital of Rajasthan?")];
const response = await model.invoke(prompt);
console.log(response);
The output will be:
Instead of a single string, chat models use different types of message objects that correspond to each role:
- HumanMessage: A message from the user’s perspective.
- AIMessage: A message from the AI’s perspective.
- SystemMessage: A message with instructions for the AI to follow.
- ChatMessage: A general-purpose message that lets you manually set a role.
Let’s try that last example again, but this time we’ll add a `SystemMessage` to give the AI a specific instruction:
import { ChatGoogleGenerativeAI } from "@langchain/google-genai";
import "dotenv/config";
import { HumanMessage, SystemMessage } from "@langchain/core/messages";const model = new ChatGoogleGenerativeAI({
model: "gemini-2.5-flash",
apiKey: process.env.GEMINI_API_KEY,
});
const prompt = [
new SystemMessage(
`You are a helpful assistant that responds to questions with chalo ab chai peelo sentence in the end.`
),
new HumanMessage("What is the capital of Jaipur?"),
];
const response = await model.invoke(prompt);
console.log(response);
Press enter or click to view image in full size

As you can see, the model follows the instruction in the `SystemMessage` even though the user’s question didn’t mention it. This is a powerful feature that lets you pre-configure your AI application to behave in a specific, predictable way based on the user’s input.
Making LLM Prompts Reusable
Example 1
Answer the question based on the context below.
If the question cannot be answered using the information provided, answer with "I don't know".Context: Python is a popular programming language known for its simplicity and readability.
It is widely used in data science, machine learning, web development, and automation.
Libraries like NumPy, Pandas, and TensorFlow have made it a preferred choice for developers.
Question: Which Python library is commonly used for numerical computations?
Answer:
Example 2
Answer the question based on the context below.
If the question cannot be answered using the information provided, answer with "I don't know".Context: Relational databases store data in tables with rows and columns, and they use
SQL (Structured Query Language) for querying. Popular relational databases include
MySQL, PostgreSQL, and Oracle Database. Non-relational databases like MongoDB
store data in flexible, JSON-like documents.
Question: Which query language is used in relational databases?
Answer:
Example 3
Answer the question based on the context below.
If the question cannot be answered using the information provided, answer with "I don't know".Context: Artificial Intelligence (AI) is a field of computer science focused on building
systems that can perform tasks requiring human intelligence. Subfields of AI include
machine learning, natural language processing, and computer vision. AI applications
are used in chatbots, recommendation systems, and self-driving cars.
Question: Name one subfield of Artificial Intelligence.
Answer:
Although the prompt looks like a simple string, the challenge is figuring out what the
text should contain and how it should vary based on the user’s input. In this example,
the Context and Question values are hardcoded, but what if we wanted to pass these
in dynamically?
Fortunately, LangChain provides prompt template interfaces that make it easy to
construct prompts with dynamic inputs:
Python Example
import { PromptTemplate } from '@langchain/core/prompts'const template = PromptTemplate.fromTemplate(`Answer the question based on the
context below. If the question cannot be answered using the information
provided, answer with "I don't know".
Context: {context}
Question: {question}
Answer:`)
await template.invoke({
context: `Python is a versatile programming language widely used in data science,
web development, and automation. It has a rich ecosystem of libraries such as
NumPy for numerical computing, Pandas for data manipulation, and TensorFlow
for building machine learning models.`,
question: `Which Python library is commonly used for data manipulation?`
})
chai Example
import { PromptTemplate } from '@langchain/core/prompts'const template = PromptTemplate.fromTemplate(`Answer the question based on the
context below. If the question cannot be answered using the information
provided, answer with "I don't know".
Context: {context}
Question: {question}
Answer:`)
await template.invoke({
context: `Chai is one of the most loved beverages in India. It is made by brewing
black tea leaves with milk, water, and spices like ginger and cardamom.
Many people enjoy chai in the evening with snacks such as pakoras or biscuits.`,
question: `Which spice is commonly added to chai for flavor?`
})
Let’s see how we’d feed this into an LLM Gemini model using LangChain
Now that we know how to build a dynamic prompt template, let’s see how to actually plug it into a Gemini model using LangChain. This is a great example of LangChain’s modular design:
1. Define the LLM
First, create an instance of the Gemini model you want to use.
We do this by importing the `ChatGoogleGenerativeAI` class from `@langchain/google-genai` and passing in the API key and model name (for example, `”gemini-2.5-flash”`).
2. Define the Template
Next, set up a `PromptTemplate`.
This template contains placeholders like `{context}` and `{question}` that can be dynamically filled with user-provided values.
3. Fill the Template
Using `.format()`, we inject the actual context and question into the template.
This produces a complete, ready-to-use prompt string.
4. Send Prompt to Gemini
Finally, we pass the generated prompt to the Gemini model’s `.invoke()` method.
The model processes it and gives us a response.
Example Code with Gemini
import "dotenv/config";
import { PromptTemplate } from "@langchain/core/prompts";
import { ChatGoogleGenerativeAI } from "@langchain/google-genai";// 1. Define the LLM
const model = new ChatGo
